category_summary:
The day's pulse centered on **AI agents** maturing into persistent, org-wide teammates, with major product launches and striking adoption metrics balanced by harder talk on cost, risk, and infrastructure.

- **Andrej Karpathy** [framed a new paradigm](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-3ee44c64cc98) for working with **Claude** as a persistent, asynchronous org-wide entity, drawing 2.4M views and the day's biggest discussion.
- **Anthropic's Boris Cherny** launched **Claude Tag**, a [proactive multiplayer Slack agent](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-b858956a9b4d) on Claude Code, claiming [65% of his team's new code](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-c0857ea5aaf1) is AI-generated.
- **Mistral AI** shipped **Mistral OCR 4** with [bounding boxes, block classification](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-643a64b6aad7), and confidence scores across 170 languages, [topping OlmOCRBench](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-3baf619268fe).

Enterprise value and operational reality drew sharp, practical takes:
- **Ethan Mollick** shared a Cornell case study where a [treasury Claude skill recovered $100k](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-f9f8d83fa21b) in back payments.
- **Santiago (Svpino)** warned that [agentic token bills are tripling](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-d960be059d4b) as autonomous agents make hidden calls, pushing LLM gateways.
- **swyx** argued **SpaceX's** combined [NeoCloud+NeoLab model is uniquely effective](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-0593803ae926) at recouping compute spend.

Technical and research threads rounded out the day:
- **Harrison Chase (LangChain)** spotlighted a [Self-Harness paper](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-90592376f9da) on agents improving their own harnesses.
- **NVIDIA** touted [powering 81% of the TOP500](/?date=2026-06-24&category=social#item-a1505d79e691) supercomputing list, underscoring its infrastructure dominance.

items.title: This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the ...
items.content:
This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the other human activity org-wide. Once you do all of the under the hood engineering work to make this "just work" (e.g. across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, security, etc.), Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way - you can talk to it as you would talk to a person and it can help with a very large variety of workloads.

Imo this is the 3rd major redesign of LLM UIUX. The first paradigm was that the LLM is a website you go to, the second was that it is an app you download to your computer. This third one is that it is a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans. It really takes a while to wrap your head around it, but it works and it is awesome.

items.title:
🙏 Thanks to the @NVIDIAAI team for highlighting DFlash support on vLLM!

With DFlash speculative dec...
items.content:
🙏 Thanks to the @NVIDIAAI team for highlighting DFlash support on vLLM!

With DFlash speculative decoding, swapping EAGLE-3 for a DFlash checkpoint is a config-only change — no code edits needed.

It runs through the open-source Speculators library, which links the DFlash drafter to the target model's hidden states in the vLLM inference path.

On Gemma-4 31B on a single Blackwell Ultra GPU, this delivers up to 5.8x higher throughput at the same concurrency over autoregressive decoding:
🧮 Math500 — 5.8x
➕ GSM8K — 5.3x
💻 HumanEval — 5.6x
🐍 MBPP — 4.4x

Read the blog here! 👇
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: The vLLM project thanks NVIDIA and details DFlash speculative decoding support, noting a config-only swap from EAGLE-3 and up to 5.8x throughput gains on Gemma-4 31B on a single Blackwell Ultra GPU.
items.reasoning: Concrete technical depth with benchmarks across multiple datasets and good engagement from a respected open-source inference project.

i dont think anyone is correctly doing the math around how SpaceX, the NeoCloud+NeoLab, is currently going to market?

SpaceX has already recouped about HALF its investment in Cursor, in compute deals. The other half is  paid for if Composer 3 does well. 

No other company is simultaneously a leading model lab + neocloud (at least where GPUs is concerned). its a crazy effective combo iff you've adequately planned out gpu supply if inhouse training 1) goes very well 2) doesn't go very well

🧠Self-Harness: Harnesses that improve themselves

New paper on agents shaping their own harnesses to...
items.content:
🧠Self-Harness: Harnesses that improve themselves

New paper on agents shaping their own harnesses to improve over time. Not from LangChain, but builds on top of DeepAgents! Three key steps:

1/ Weakness mining: find failure modes from traces
2/ Harness proposal: suggest changes to the harness
3/ Proposal validation: does regression testing on proposals and then accepts

Paper: https://t.co/yDd4TwPKwl

Builds on top of DeepAgents: https://t.co/a3Dv7ZVaSy

items.title:
The token bill of one of my clients tripled over the last couple of weeks.

There was nothing broken...
items.content:
The token bill of one of my clients tripled over the last couple of weeks.

There was nothing broken.

The better these agents get, the more tokens they are eating.

You ask your agent to refactor a function. It looks like a simple request, but behind the scenes, the agent is making a dozen calls:

• Reading files
• Pulling relevant context
• Planning the changes
• Writing the code
• Checking any errors
• Updating the tests
• Retrying anything that failed

Every call costs money. The more autonomous these agents get, the more we'll pay for them.

Multiply that across a few teams, and your bill will blow up.

Worst part of all of this:

It's really hard to know who is spending the money, on which models, and for which tasks.

Nobody sees where all the money is really going.

The solution:

You need a gateway between your agents and the model providers.

You want every request to pass through your gateway first. No exceptions.

A gateway buys you freedom, visibility, observability, flexibility, control, and a bunch of other benefits.

More importantly, it lets you see spending, route cheap work to cheap models, and keep the expensive models for the hard tasks.

I work with @orq_ai. They route to 500+ models across 30+ providers behind a single API.

Check this out: https://t.co/akGVJVV3zW

Thanks to the @orq_ai team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: AI in 2040 will not be built on the stack we are using today. It will be much closer to optimal. The...
items.content:
AI in 2040 will not be built on the stack we are using today. It will be much closer to optimal. The current stack has 3-4 orders of magnitude of data inefficiency and 4-5 orders of magnitude of compute inefficiency.

Near-optimal AI is what symbolic learning will deliver.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Francois Chollet predicts that 2040 AI will not be built on today's stack, citing 3-4 orders of magnitude data inefficiency and 4-5 orders compute inefficiency, and argues symbolic learning will deliver near-optimal AI.
items.reasoning: Bold long-horizon prediction from a prominent researcher tying to his symbolic-learning thesis; strong engagement and conceptual depth.

items.title:
Wild story on AI + diagnostics via the New York Times: 

In 2025, 45-year-old security guard showed ...
items.content:
Wild story on AI + diagnostics via the New York Times: 

In 2025, 45-year-old security guard showed up at a Queens ER, coughing up blood and struggling to breathe.

Chest X-ray: clean. Electrocardiogram: abnormal, but nothing pointing to a diagnosis. 

They learned he'd recently been near wildfire smoke, and gave him asthma meds and an inhaler.

But an AI system called EchoNext was scanning every ECG in that hospital system in the background, and flagged possible severe heart damage.

They called him back a week later. His heart was pumping out just 10% of its blood with each beat, and a valve was leaking. 

The cause turned out to be a rare genetic disorder linked to sudden death, and he needed a transplant to survive.

EchoNext is FDA-cleared, and will be launching free inside OpenEvidence, a medical chatbot already used by about half the doctors in the US.

items.reasoning: Compelling, concrete real-world AI diagnostics case with named tools and FDA clearance, though sourced from a newsletter recap.

Wild story on AI + diagnostics via the New York Times:

In 2025, 45-year-old security guard showed up at a Queens ER, coughing up blood and struggling to breathe.

Chest X-ray: clean. Electrocardiogram: abnormal, but nothing pointing to a diagnosis.

They learned he'd recently been near wildfire smoke, and gave him asthma meds and an inhaler.

But an AI system called EchoNext was scanning every ECG in that hospital system in the background, and flagged possible severe heart damage.

They called him back a week later. His heart was pumping out just 10% of its blood with each beat, and a valve was leaking.

The cause turned out to be a rare genetic disorder linked to sudden death, and he needed a transplant to survive.

EchoNext is FDA-cleared, and will be launching free inside OpenEvidence, a medical chatbot already used by about half the doctors in the US.

items.title: New lecture for the book! Nominally about synthetic data, but mostly is a walk through of the distil...
items.content:
New lecture for the book! Nominally about synthetic data, but mostly is a walk through of the distillation literature from the Hinton 2015 paper to multi-teach on-policy distillation of today!

At 7.4 hours of video in my post-training brain dump and counting :)

It was fun to stare at the math long enough and talk through the 3-4 core changes that needed to be made to the original formulation to have on-policy distillation be ready for the mainstream like it is today (and in RL frameworks).

Otherwise, I include a bit of a history lesson for how synthetic data generally slowly took over all post-training data research (it wasn't always the case)! Then I do some 101 review on constitutional AI, rubrics, and other popular methods.

00:00 The emergence of synthetic data
10:50 Background on teacher-student knowledge-distillation
24:47: On-policy distillation (OPD, MOPD, and OPSD)
37:11 Constitutional AI & AI Feedback
45:50 Rubrics as rewards & conclusions

Ofc, watch on YouTube etc.

items.content: With agentic coding, complexity compounds in a mechanical way: unnecessary code ends up in the codebase, moves to the context window, degrades the model's reasoning abilities, leads to more unnecessary code (often to fix issues arising from the unnecessary code). It's exponential

items.title:
/2 MiniMax opens the model weights of Minimax M3 to the public.

M3 is the first open-weight model t...
items.content:
/2 MiniMax opens the model weights of Minimax M3 to the public.

M3 is the first open-weight model to combine frontier coding, a 1 million token context window, and native multimodal support (images and video) in one package. That combo was locked behind paid APIs until now.

The 1M context window is the big deal. A new sparse attention design drops the compute cost at 1M context to roughly 1/20th of the previous generation, so it does not cost a fortune to run long contexts.

On the coding side: M3 scores 59.0% on SWE-Bench Pro, ahead of GPT-5.5 at 58.6%. 

Link: https://t.co/hlsW5PvbeU

items.summary: AlphaSignal reports MiniMax open-sourced M3 weights, the first open model combining frontier coding, 1M-token context with a sparse attention design (~1/20th compute), and native multimodal support, scoring 59.0% on SWE-Bench Pro versus GPT-5.5 at 58.6%.

M3 is the first open-weight model to combine frontier coding, a 1 million token context window, and native multimodal support (images and video) in one package. That combo was locked behind paid APIs until now.

The 1M context window is the big deal. A new sparse attention design drops the compute cost at 1M context to roughly 1/20th of the previous generation, so it does not cost a fortune to run long contexts.

items.title: One thing we’ve seen pretty consistently: shipping the first version is only a small part of the wor...
items.content:
One thing we’ve seen pretty consistently: shipping the first version is only a small part of the work

A key part of building reliable agents is having a repeatable lifecycle for improving them over time

Here’s what that looks like:

1/ Build. Start with the agent, tools, context, prompts, and workflows needed to get something useful in front of users

2/ Test. Use evals to understand whether the agent is doing the right thing, not just returning something that looks right

3/ Deploy. Get agents into production environments where they can actually be used

4/ Monitor. Look at traces to understand what the agent actually did: what tools it called, what context it used, where it failed

5/ Improve. Take what you learn from real usage and turn it into better prompts, better tools, better evals, and better agents

We think this type of lifecycle is something every team building agents will need

I’m going to walk through this in more detail on June 24 in The Agent Development Lifecycle 101

Sign up here: https://t.co/x2e75staG6
items.author: @hwchase17
items.summary: Harrison Chase outlines a five-step agent development lifecycle: build, test with evals, deploy, monitor traces, and improve, previewing a talk on the topic.
items.reasoning: Substantive framework from the LangChain founder on building reliable agents, though somewhat promotional for an event.

items.title: /1 https://t.co/fYXIemK5se open-source coding model with 30% fewer reasoning tokens and 21% benchmar...
items.content:
/1 https://t.co/fYXIemK5se open-source coding model with 30% fewer reasoning tokens and 21% benchmark gains.

Kimi-K2.7-Code is a new open-source coding model, and it's a meaningful step up from K2.6.

The big story here is efficiency. The model uses 30% fewer reasoning tokens than before, meaning it thinks less but gets more done. Less wasted compute, faster answers. 

Under the hood, it's a 1 trillion parameter Mixture-of-Experts model, but only 32B parameters activate per request, keeping it fast. The context window is 256K tokens.

You can run it today via the Kimi API, or self-host using vLLM or SGLang. Weights are on HuggingFace under a modified MIT license. 

Link: https://t.co/ZJCWcQfhHQ

items.summary: AlphaSignal reports Kimi-K2.7-Code, a new open-source 1T-parameter MoE coding model (32B active, 256K context) with 30% fewer reasoning tokens and 21% benchmark gains over K2.6, available via API or self-host.

Also added Waymo and Tesla FSD to https://t.co/7sQDNqbZhr

Interestingly, Waymo never had fatalities yet

And Tesla FSD is ~4x safer than the average car (in fatal odds per trip)

1,190,000 people die every year in traffic deaths

80% of those would be prevented with self driving, meaning you could save 952,000 lives every year if all cars became self driving overnight!

After heart disease and cancer, traffic deaths are the biggest killer, so it should be one of the government's biggest priorities to roll out fast!

And Tesla FSD is ~4x safer than the average car (in fatal odds per trip)

1,190,000 people die every year in traffic deaths

80% of those would be prevented with self driving, meaning you could save 952,000 lives every year if all cars became self driving overnight!

After heart disease and cancer, traffic deaths are the biggest killer, so it should be one of the government's biggest priorities to roll out fast!

For our free newsletter this week, we write about the humanoid robot safety race.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
Humanoid robots are moving closer to real-world use, but safety will matter more than impressive demos. To be trusted in factories, warehouses, hospitals, and public spaces, robots need strong testing, safety software, clear standards, and predictable behavior around people. The companies that prove their robots can operate safely and reliably will have the real advantage.

Read and subscribe: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title: HF is quietly becoming the best place to store data, public AND private, especially for brutal domai...
items.content:
HF is quietly becoming the best place to store data, public AND private, especially for brutal domains like robotics and video AI where the files are massive, append-only, and never stop growing.

Example? Public robotics datasets exploded from 1,000 in early 2025 to 60,000 today, and there's twice as many private ones.

Why? A single robot records at 140 MB/s, all day, forever. That data has to be stored, streamed to GPUs, and shipped back to hardware on repeat. Get it wrong and your GPUs sit idle at 0 MB/s waiting for a dataset to land. Get it right (stream straight from the Hub, pre-warmed cache) and those same GPUs scream along at ~1,326 MB/s, fully fed. 🚀

Here's how LeRobot + Hugging Face Storage Buckets pull it off: https://t.co/SOUPAUpiZc

items.content:
/4 Sakana AI just released Fugu, a new model API built around autonomous model orchestration. 

The stronger version, Fugu Ultra, is built for harder multi-step tasks. It scores 73.7 on SWE Bench Pro, 82.1 on TerminalBench 2.1, 93.2 on LiveCodeBench, 50.0 on Humanity’s Last Exam, and 95.5 on GPQA-D.

It also uses an OpenAI-compatible API, with Fugu Ultra priced at $5 per 1M input tokens and $30 per 1M output tokens. Above 272K context, that rises to $10 input and $45 output. 

Link: https://t.co/REWYsqxWyz

items.title:
/3 https://t.co/Scz5EoTyEi drops GLM-5.2 weights on Hugging Face.

GLM-5.2 is a flagship open-weight...
items.content:
/3 https://t.co/Scz5EoTyEi drops GLM-5.2 weights on Hugging Face.

GLM-5.2 is a flagship open-weight model built for long-horizon tasks, especially coding and agentic work. Its biggest headline is a stable 1M-token context window, giving it room to handle large codebases and longer workflows. 

The model adds flexible thinking modes, stronger benchmark performance, and architecture changes like IndexShare to reduce long-context compute. 

https://t.co/Scz5EoTyEi is also releasing it under an MIT license, with local deployment support through frameworks like SGLang, vLLM, Transformers, xLLM, and KTransformers. 

Link: https://t.co/la8mRVdXs5

items.title:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation helps AI move from generic answers to business-aware responses.

It d...
items.content:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation helps AI move from generic answers to business-aware responses.

It does not put all company knowledge inside the model, but connects AI to approved sources so answers can be grounded, traceable, and more useful at work. 

Microblog  @antgrasso #RAG

RAG is easier to understand through a simple business flow.

A user asks about a policy, a customer case, an order, or an internal procedure.

The system searches approved company sources instead of relying only on the model’s general knowledge.

Relevant passages or records are retrieved and used to shape the answer.

The result is an AI response that reflects company-specific context, with clearer references and better control.

This matters for enterprises because useful AI needs more than fluency. It needs access to trusted knowledge, secure permissions, and answers that people can verify.

Many enterprise AI systems already support this through connectors or knowledge grounding. The real work is configuring them properly, choosing reliable sources, and making sure access rules are respected.
items.author: @antgrasso
items.summary: Antonio Grasso explains Retrieval-Augmented Generation, describing how it grounds AI answers in approved company sources for traceable, business-aware responses.
items.reasoning: Clear educational microblog on RAG with strong engagement (69 likes, 29 retweets); useful explainer though aimed at a general business audience.

items.title: One of the best use cases for agentic LLMs is to use them as hyperparameter tuners for machine learn...
items.content:
One of the best use cases for agentic LLMs is to use them as hyperparameter tuners for machine learning model training experiments.

Previously, there were some external libraries based on statistical properties of the hyperparameter value distributions and the training results, but they were unreliable and hacky. You needed to tune those libraries before they could tune your model.

Now you just verbally explain the goal to the agent and it trains/analyzes the logs/resamples/retrains until it reaches some acceptable performance level.

It doesn't mean that the LLM will always make the right decisions, but those external libraries were just plain dumb.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov argues that a strong use case for agentic LLMs is hyperparameter tuning for ML training, replacing brittle statistical libraries by letting an agent iteratively train, analyze logs, resample, and retrain toward a goal.
items.reasoning: Concrete practitioner insight on a real agentic-AI workflow with decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov argues that a strong use case for agentic LLMs is hyperparameter tuning for ML training, replacing brittle statistical libraries by letting an agent iteratively train, analyze logs, resample, and retrain toward a goal.

items.title:
Increase inference performance by up to 15x without sacrificing responsiveness. 

DFlash, an open so...
items.content:
Increase inference performance by up to 15x without sacrificing responsiveness. 

DFlash, an open source lightweight block diffusion model designed for speculative decoding, delivers up to 15x higher throughput on NVIDIA Blackwell while maintaining the same user interactivity target.

Instead of drafting tokens one at a time, it proposes a whole block in a single pass for the main model to verify in parallel.

Adoption is drop-in with support in @lmsysorg SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, and @vllm_project.

Free and open frontier AI is the first truly communist gift China has given to humanity.

It's the f...
items.content:
Free and open frontier AI is the first truly communist gift China has given to humanity.

It's the first such gift since the Soviet Union gave the workers of the world gender equality, an 8-hour workday, a month of vacation, and social security.

Without China, three fat U.S. capitalists would still be selling us GPT-3.7 with ads.

items.title:
Claude Code can parse the whole Internet using Apify actors.

And with MCP connectors, you can talk ...
items.content:
Claude Code can parse the whole Internet using Apify actors.

And with MCP connectors, you can talk to servers and have your agents perform operations behind paywalls.

Example (see the video here): Analyze a YouTube video and write the summary in a Notion page.

Another example: Scrape the school calendar and automatically add the events to your Google Calendar.

You can do all of this using Claude Code + Apify Skills.

Setting this up takes a few seconds:

• Open your Apify settings > API & Integrations
• Connect your tool (Notion, Google Calendar, ...)
• Authorize once

Your actors will now have direct read and write access to those services.

Here is a blog post with an explanation of how MCP connectors work in Apify: https://t.co/fjyHSTAx9O

Thanks to the Apify team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
The new company brain.

 Context is the soul of your company, I learned today.

Since I wrote a book...
items.content:
The new company brain.

 Context is the soul of your company, I learned today.

Since I wrote a book, “Age of Context,” years ago I see that context is what we now call our private data.

That data should be stored in a way to let AI agents and humans get more and more use out of it. 

This is precisely what @PromptQL does and here @tanmaigo goes deep on how it now is a company brain, or, rather, a wiki that everyone updates and uses.

items.reasoning: Thematic insight on context/knowledge management for agents, tying to his Age of Context framing; decent reach but promotional.

That data should be stored in a way to let AI agents and humans get more and more use out of it.

This is precisely what @PromptQL does and here @tanmaigo goes deep on how it now is a company brain, or, rather, a wiki that everyone updates and uses.

items.title: When I first saw augmented reality properly demonstrated in the parking lot of a German startup, the...
items.content:
When I first saw augmented reality properly demonstrated in the parking lot of a German startup, the CTO of Metaio showed me monsters on the sides of skyscrapers that were across the parking lot. 

That was pretty cool. 

He later set up Apple for decades with his Spatial Computing strategy.

This gives you a little taste of why everybody is going to buy glasses soon: when you can live in this kind of infinite reality world, it will be highly entertaining.

Consider Formula 1.

When someone is sitting next to you wearing them during a race and then looks at you in almost a disgust: “dude, put them on. You can see the whole track in real time.”

You will have a pair the next race.

I had a similar demo at NASCAR.

The autonomous racing league in Abu Dhabi gave me a demonstration on its Formula 1 track.

The richest in the world can’t see the whole track from their seats.

This sure seems like a problem worth solving.

Do you have any clue what would happen if Max Verstappen joined with Apple?

And put you in the car at 250 m.p.h. In a world-class competition. Digitally.

Once you get 10 minutes in the glasses that soon will come. Some say @Snap is already there. You will buy even if you are blind.

I once went to a dinner for the school for the blind.

Every augmented reality team was there. Everyone knows this tech can bring sight to the blind.

It brings it to all of us.

Put it on and you who see you’re entire world differently.

I argue better.

I was the first to tell you about the World Wide Telescope that was launched by a small team working at Microsoft Research.

The technologies that are coming soon will let you actually see every single object in the sky.

It is an infinite discovery platform.

Create any reality you want at a decent price.

Stay alive. You will want to experience it. 

Even if you go to every race in a private jet.

Everyone can build together. 

Which will take us out of this shitty mood everyone is in about AI. https://t.co/2QqaqqihiD

items.title: Market efficiency is a myth. Markets are made of people, who are irrational both individually and in...
items.content:
Market efficiency is a myth. Markets are made of people, who are irrational both individually and in aggregate.

Maybe if human irrationality were completely random, it might average out to a rational whole. But in practice individual irrationality is driven by globally shared irrational narratives -- almost everybody is an irrational conformist.

items.title: Noam Shazeer, co-author of "Attention Is All You Need," departed Google to join OpenAI, while his st...
items.content:
Noam Shazeer, co-author of "Attention Is All You Need," departed Google to join OpenAI, while his startup, https://t.co/kreHWd6r6U, achieved a $1 billion valuation in March 2023. Despite the company’s difficulties in establishing sustainable subscription revenues, Shazeer’s engineering expertise attracted immense interest from investors and tech titans alike. In 2024, Google spent $2.7 billion to license https://t.co/kreHWd6r6U’s technology and re-hire Shazeer. Meanwhile, Nobel laureate John Jumper announced his departure from Google DeepMind to join Anthropic.

This wave of high-profile talent departures ignited this week a seismic response on Wall Street contributing to the erasure of $1.5 trillion in market capitalization among major tech firms Mag 7.

Nvidia -$85B   
Amazon -$173B   
Meta -$70B   
Alphabet -225B   
Microsoft -$189B   
Tesla -$199B   
SPCX  -$600B

https://t.co/tsaUrmBhxx
https://t.co/gs7HNAMHHK

1⃣ The failing grade usually isn't servers lying — it's them claiming they support ...
items.content:
What stood out:

1⃣ The failing grade usually isn't servers lying — it's them claiming they support everything, then falling over when you invoke any of it. The fix is boring (cover the protocol, report capabilities honestly), which is probably why so few people bother.

items.title:
We don't need another dashboard.

Nobody has time to check a dashboard to see if their agents are wo...
items.content:
We don't need another dashboard.

Nobody has time to check a dashboard to see if their agents are working.

A dashboard is more work. It's one more thing to check, one more place to look, one more problem on my plate.

I don't want more work. Just tell me what's broken.

We need insights, not dashboards.

I'm glad these guys are focusing on giving people answers instead of building yet another dashboard.

items.title: @notch If you are asking “Why push back against anti-datacenter efforts?” I consider it a tragedy th...
items.content:
@notch If you are asking “Why push back against anti-datacenter efforts?” I consider it a tragedy that anti-nuclear efforts largely strangled nuclear power in the US based on vibes, and I don’t want to see that happen to AI. Public opinion matters, and it shouldn’t be ceded unchallenged.

If you are asking “Why should I support AI efforts at all?” I believe we are in the midst of a transition more vibrant than the industrial revolution. Opinions formed a couple of years ago about the uselessness of AI are no longer valid. Millions of people and organizations are getting great returns from using it, and the demand for data centers is the market responding to the value signal. That is how progress is made!

items.title: That matters if you're building on top of MCP, because a server that misreports its capabilities and...
items.content: That matters if you're building on top of MCP, because a server that misreports its capabilities and then crashes mid-call looks like your agent getting worse, not the server. Hard to debug when there are four hops between your client and the thing that broke.

items.title: /5 NVIDIA Research introduced ArtiFixer, a SIGGRAPH 2026 project for improving weak 3D reconstructio...
items.content:
/5 NVIDIA Research introduced ArtiFixer, a SIGGRAPH 2026 project for improving weak 3D reconstructions.

It fixes a common 3D Gaussian Splatting problem: scenes look good from captured views but break in unseen areas.

ArtiFixer uses auto-regressive diffusion to generate missing views, then uses them as pseudo-supervision.

NVIDIA says it beats prior methods by 1–3 dB PSNR. 

Link: https://t.co/U7DR4IWEVH
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal summarizes NVIDIA's ArtiFixer (SIGGRAPH 2026), which uses auto-regressive diffusion to generate missing views as pseudo-supervision for weak 3D reconstructions, claiming 1-3 dB PSNR gains.
items.reasoning: Concise technical recap of a notable research project, but near-zero engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal summarizes NVIDIA's ArtiFixer (SIGGRAPH 2026), which uses auto-regressive diffusion to generate missing views as pseudo-supervision for weak 3D reconstructions, claiming 1-3 dB PSNR gains.
items.content_html:
/5 NVIDIA Research introduced ArtiFixer, a SIGGRAPH 2026 project for improving weak 3D reconstructions.

It fixes a common 3D Gaussian Splatting problem: scenes look good from captured views but break in unseen areas.

ArtiFixer uses auto-regressive diffusion to generate missing views, then uses them as pseudo-supervision.

NVIDIA says it beats prior methods by 1–3 dB PSNR.

Link: https://t.co/U7DR4IWEVH

items.title: Bidirectional video models provide great coherence but are computationally heavy. ArtiFixer solves t...
items.content:
Bidirectional video models provide great coherence but are computationally heavy. ArtiFixer solves this via Self-Forcing-style Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD).

By distilling the bidirectional model into a causal auto-regressive one, ArtiFixer achieves up to a 70x speedup over base models. It can generate clean, plausible content in as few as 1 to 4 denoising steps without accumulating progressive drift
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains ArtiFixer uses Self-Forcing-style Distribution Matching Distillation to convert a bidirectional video model into a causal one for up to 70x speedup in 1-4 denoising steps.
items.reasoning: Genuine technical detail on distillation, but minimal engagement within a thread.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal explains ArtiFixer uses Self-Forcing-style Distribution Matching Distillation to convert a bidirectional video model into a causal one for up to 70x speedup in 1-4 denoising steps.
items.content_html:
Bidirectional video models provide great coherence but are computationally heavy. ArtiFixer solves this via Self-Forcing-style Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD).

By distilling the bidirectional model into a causal auto-regressive one, ArtiFixer achieves up to a 70x speedup over base models. It can generate clean, plausible content in as few as 1 to 4 denoising steps without accumulating progressive drift

items.title:
If you know we are screwed, well then, can't we unscrew ourselves?

Life is pretty good in my house ...
items.content:
If you know we are screwed, well then, can't we unscrew ourselves?

Life is pretty good in my house tonight. The trick is to spread it to everybody.

I see the opportunities that are about to be opened up, but it's hard to explain it to even nerds. It means we're all playing some sort of virtual Burning Man kind of game all day long, where we just turn the entire world into Burning Man—a lot of fun all the time. 

I see that future on the other side of this. The trick is: how are we going to cross the bridge?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Optimistic futurist essay imagining an AI-driven world functioning like a perpetual virtual Burning Man, while asking how society crosses the transition.
items.reasoning: Strong engagement including many replies and a substantive, if speculative, vision of an AI-abundant future from a high-reach futurist.
items.summary_html: Optimistic futurist essay imagining an AI-driven world functioning like a perpetual virtual Burning Man, while asking how society crosses the transition.

I see the opportunities that are about to be opened up, but it's hard to explain it to even nerds. It means we're all playing some sort of virtual Burning Man kind of game all day long, where we just turn the entire world into Burning Man—a lot of fun all the time.

I see that future on the other side of this. The trick is: how are we going to cross the bridge?

items.title:
ArtiFixer doesn't just clean up 3D meshes—it is a robust generative engine.

Even if you entirely dr...
items.content:
ArtiFixer doesn't just clean up 3D meshes—it is a robust generative engine.

Even if you entirely drop the initial 3D rendering conditions, the model can rely on text prompts or just a few reference views to reconstruct the high-level structure of the scene and synthesize coherent, accurate video.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal describes ArtiFixer as a generative engine that can reconstruct 3D scene structure from text prompts or sparse reference views even without initial 3D rendering conditions.
items.reasoning: Part of a technical thread on NVIDIA research, but near-zero engagement and fragmentary.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal describes ArtiFixer as a generative engine that can reconstruct 3D scene structure from text prompts or sparse reference views even without initial 3D rendering conditions.
items.content_html:
ArtiFixer doesn't just clean up 3D meshes—it is a robust generative engine.

Even if you entirely drop the initial 3D rendering conditions, the model can rely on text prompts or just a few reference views to reconstruct the high-level structure of the scene and synthesize coherent, accurate video.

items.title: The results are incredibly sharp. When benchmarked on challenging datasets with sparse views like Mi...
items.content:
The results are incredibly sharp. When benchmarked on challenging datasets with sparse views like Mip-NeRF 360 and DL3DV, ArtiFixer handles highly degraded initial renderings effortlessly.

It outperforms existing baselines (like GenFusion and 3DGUT) by a massive 1–3 dB PSNR margin, properly reconstructing empty regions that other models fail to fill.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal claims ArtiFixer outperforms baselines like GenFusion and 3DGUT by 1-3 dB PSNR on sparse-view datasets such as Mip-NeRF 360 and DL3DV.
items.reasoning: Specific benchmark claims but essentially no engagement and a continuation of a thread.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal claims ArtiFixer outperforms baselines like GenFusion and 3DGUT by 1-3 dB PSNR on sparse-view datasets such as Mip-NeRF 360 and DL3DV.
items.content_html:
The results are incredibly sharp. When benchmarked on challenging datasets with sparse views like Mip-NeRF 360 and DL3DV, ArtiFixer handles highly degraded initial renderings effortlessly.

It outperforms existing baselines (like GenFusion and 3DGUT) by a massive 1–3 dB PSNR margin, properly reconstructing empty regions that other models fail to fill.

items.title: How do you keep the generated video consistent with existing views without losing the ability to hal...
items.content:
How do you keep the generated video consistent with existing views without losing the ability to hallucinate new content?

The answer is Opacity Mixing. Instead of starting from pure noise, ArtiFixer:
> Downscales the rendering's opacity map.
> Mixes Gaussian noise specifically into low-opacity (unseen) regions.
> Prevents mode collapse and preserves generative capacity for entirely novel areas
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal details ArtiFixer's Opacity Mixing technique, which injects noise into low-opacity unseen regions to preserve generative capacity and prevent mode collapse.
items.reasoning: Specific method explanation but negligible engagement in a thread.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal details ArtiFixer's Opacity Mixing technique, which injects noise into low-opacity unseen regions to preserve generative capacity and prevent mode collapse.
items.content_html:
How do you keep the generated video consistent with existing views without losing the ability to hallucinate new content?

The answer is Opacity Mixing. Instead of starting from pure noise, ArtiFixer:

> Downscales the rendering's opacity map.

> Mixes Gaussian noise specifically into low-opacity (unseen) regions.

> Prevents mode collapse and preserves generative capacity for entirely novel areas

items.title: Instead of treating 3D reconstruction and video generation as standalone alternatives, ArtiFixer com...
items.content:
Instead of treating 3D reconstruction and video generation as standalone alternatives, ArtiFixer combines their strengths.

> Phase I: Trains a powerful bidirectional generative video model to transport degraded renderings into clean frames.
> Phase II: Distills the teacher model into a rapid, auto-regressive generator capable of producing hundreds of frames in a single pass.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal outlines ArtiFixer's two-phase pipeline: a bidirectional video model to clean degraded renderings, then distillation into a fast auto-regressive generator.
items.reasoning: Architecture summary with little engagement, part of the same thread.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal outlines ArtiFixer's two-phase pipeline: a bidirectional video model to clean degraded renderings, then distillation into a fast auto-regressive generator.
items.content_html:
Instead of treating 3D reconstruction and video generation as standalone alternatives, ArtiFixer combines their strengths.

> Phase I: Trains a powerful bidirectional generative video model to transport degraded renderings into clean frames.

> Phase II: Distills the teacher model into a rapid, auto-regressive generator capable of producing hundreds of frames in a single pass.

items.title: @QuantumTumbler @PaulSkallas I don't care about whether it solves death or not, but my dad had a kid...
items.content:
@QuantumTumbler @PaulSkallas I don't care about whether it solves death or not, but my dad had a kidney transplant and he had to take drugs every day for the rest of his life to keep his body from rejecting the organ. 

This new startup keeps you from having to do that, which is a great improvement. We should celebrate great improvements. We don't have to go all the way to saving lives; there are a lot of things to be done in healthcare just to have great improvements.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble discusses a startup whose technology prevents organ transplant rejection, removing the need for lifelong immunosuppressant drugs, framed as a worthwhile healthcare improvement.
items.reasoning: Personal anecdote on biotech healthcare innovation from a credible commentator; only tangentially AI-related and modest engagement.
items.summary_html: Scoble discusses a startup whose technology prevents organ transplant rejection, removing the need for lifelong immunosuppressant drugs, framed as a worthwhile healthcare improvement.
items.content_html:
@QuantumTumbler @PaulSkallas I don't care about whether it solves death or not, but my dad had a kidney transplant and he had to take drugs every day for the rest of his life to keep his body from rejecting the organ.

This new startup keeps you from having to do that, which is a great improvement. We should celebrate great improvements. We don't have to go all the way to saving lives; there are a lot of things to be done in healthcare just to have great improvements.

items.title: 50 ML projects to understand LLMs — Investigate transformer mechanisms through data analysis, visual...
items.content:
50 ML projects to understand LLMs — Investigate transformer mechanisms through data analysis, visualization, & experimentation: https://t.co/mp3s7JFolp v/ @PacktDataML

This book perfectly reflects its title "50 ML Projects to Understand LLMs". The entire book consists of exactly that: 50 projects, with tasks and subtasks pleasantly outlined, explained, and presented in a beautifully instructive and consistent style. No extraneous pedagogical content. The learning experience in each project delivers the promised understanding of LLM concepts, including informative graphics and detailed elaboration on the project's subtasks as needed. This book is essential for anyone seeking to build LLMs with confidence, knowledge, and skill.

items.title: The enterprise question coming out of the PGA Championship is not “what technology did T-Mobile depl...
items.content:
The enterprise question coming out of the PGA Championship is not “what technology did T-Mobile deploy?”

It is: is your network built to support multiple systems simultaneously, under pressure, at scale?

Because the network is no longer background infrastructure.

It is part of the operational infrastructure behind the experience.

I broke down what that looked like across broadcast, operations, fan experience, and workforce in my latest video with @TMobileBusiness.

Watch it here: https://t.co/8l1WLRTZ5A

What would it take for your organization’s network to support this kind of operational demand? Drop your thoughts below.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title:
Then there are the layers most people overlook.

→ 5G Transact supporting payments across the venue
...
items.content:
Then there are the layers most people overlook.

→ 5G Transact supporting payments across the venue
→ SuperMobile helping keep the workforce connected
→ The AI Assistant in the PGA app supporting fan questions

Different systems. Different users. One connected operational environment.

This is not about any single technology. It is about what becomes possible when multiple systems operate on network infrastructure designed to support concurrent demand.

Network slicing means dedicated capacity for AI computer vision, simultaneously supporting tens of thousands of fan devices, without one system slowing down another.

That is enterprise infrastructure design in practice. And it is a direct CIO conversation.

items.title: Most people think the network is background infrastructure at a live sports event. The PGA Champions...
items.content:
Most people think the network is background infrastructure at a live sports event. The PGA Championship just proved that thinking is already obsolete.

As a T-Mobile for Business Partner, I was on site across 300+ acres where broadcast, operations, fan experience, workforce, and commerce all had to run simultaneously across a highly demanding operational environment.

What I saw changed how I think about enterprise infrastructure entirely.

A thread on how connected event infrastructure is evolving

———
Amazon Summary:

"This book shows you step-b...
items.content:
Build AI-Enhanced Web Apps: https://t.co/YkL2dtkJSf
———
Amazon Summary:

"This book shows you step-by-step and example-by-example how to build sites and applications that take advantage of large language models (LLMs) like GPT, Claude, and Llama. Written especially for web developers comfortable with React or Next.js, this book introduces the tools and techniques you need to add sophisticated AI features like Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), document summarization, chatbots, and more to your web-based projects.

"It guides you through AI development using only JavaScript and other common web dev skills–no Python or Machine Learning experience required. You’ll learn by working with full-scale AI projects that solve actual business problems. You’ll soon be delivering user-friendly, efficient interfaces that make the absolute best use of AI tech."

This LLM book is for database developers and data scientists w...
items.content:
Available at https://t.co/uwuobKTPm3

This LLM book is for database developers and data scientists who want to leverage knowledge graphs with Neo4j and its vector search capabilities to build intelligent search and recommendation systems. Working knowledge of Python and Java is essential to follow along. Familiarity with Neo4j, the Cypher query language, and fundamental concepts of databases will come in handy.

𝓦𝓱𝓪𝓽 𝓨𝓸𝓾 𝓦𝓲𝓵𝓵 𝓛𝓮𝓪𝓻𝓷:
🟠Design, populate, and integrate a Neo4j knowledge graph with RAG
🔵Model data for knowledge graphs
🟢Integrate AI-powered search to enhance knowledge exploration
🟠Maintain and monitor your AI search application with Haystack
🔵Use LangChain4j and Spring AI for recommendations and personalization
🟢Seamlessly deploy your applications to Google Cloud Platform

items.title:
From @PacktDataML — available at https://t.co/fmHgkRo26I

"AI-Native LLM Security — Threats, Defense...
items.content:
From @PacktDataML — available at https://t.co/fmHgkRo26I

"AI-Native LLM Security — Threats, Defenses, and Best Practices for Building Safe and Trustworthy AI"

𝘽𝙤𝙤𝙠 𝘿𝙚𝙨𝙘𝙧𝙞𝙥𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣:
"Adversarial AI attacks present a unique set of security challenges, exploiting the very foundation of how AI learns. This book explores these threats in depth, equipping cybersecurity professionals with the tools needed to secure generative AI and LLM applications. Rather than skimming the surface of emerging risks, it focuses on practical strategies, industry standards, and recent research to build a robust defense framework.

Structured around actionable insights, the chapters introduce a secure-by-design methodology, integrating threat modeling and MLSecOps practices to fortify AI systems. You’ll discover how to leverage established taxonomies from OWASP, NIST, and MITRE to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities. Through real-world examples, the book highlights best practices for incorporating security controls into AI development life cycles, covering key areas such as CI/CD, MLOps, and open-access LLMs."

items.title: Hey, @elonmusk, could you please address this injustice on X already? When someone replies to your p...
items.content: Hey, @elonmusk, could you please address this injustice on X already? When someone replies to your post or comment with an insult and then blocks you so you cannot tell them who they are, it's unfair. It's my comment, and they replied to it. I must be able to reply. Otherwise, it's like kicking someone in the back and hiding behind a closed door. Unfair.

items.title:
Now zoom out to operations.

Running a 300-acre event means managing crowd movement, entry points, c...
items.content:
Now zoom out to operations.

Running a 300-acre event means managing crowd movement, entry points, concessions, connected devices, and staff coordination simultaneously.

The Operations Command Center brought that fragmented operational data into real-time operational visibility.

Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, the platform supported operational workflows across the venue.

Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.

items.title: Let’s start with broadcast, because this is where the shift is most visible. The T-Mobile Range Show...
items.content:
Let’s start with broadcast, because this is where the shift is most visible. The T-Mobile Range Show is not a demo. It is a live production environment.

What it runs on:

→ 5G On Demand powering roaming cameras across the course → A mobile 4D capture system delivering broadcast-quality content without permanent infrastructure → 360 degree swing analysis streamed in real time

The network is not supporting the broadcast.
The network became part of the production infrastructure.

items.content:
@roboindexio @virtuals_io @eastworlds_io That's just the start. Soon, with a pair of augmented reality glasses on, you can change the clothing on your robot in real time, along with many other things the robot is doing or the worlds it's interacting with.

There are no rules to this future. It's a digital future, and anybody who says there are any limitations on what humans can do visually or in audio just hasn't really done their homework.

items.title:
Most AI systems still rely on centralized processing:
 
→ Data is sent
 → Processed
 → Returned
 
Th...
items.content:
Most AI systems still rely on centralized processing:
 
→ Data is sent
 → Processed
 → Returned
 
That model works, but for time-sensitive use cases, responsiveness and control become critical.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Sponsored thread point noting most AI systems rely on centralized processing, which limits responsiveness for time-sensitive cases.
items.reasoning: States a real edge-vs-cloud tradeoff but within a promotional thread with low reach.
items.summary_html: Sponsored thread point noting most AI systems rely on centralized processing, which limits responsiveness for time-sensitive cases.
items.content_html:
Most AI systems still rely on centralized processing:

→ Data is sent

→ Processed

→ Returned

That model works, but for time-sensitive use cases, responsiveness and control become critical.

Second, we all know that many countries do have rules forbidding lowering the indoor temperature below some ridiculous threshold. Third, you don't need to force plebs to do something. You can simply make it too expensive or you can shame them and the plebs will choose to do that on their own.

items.title: Only about 25% of French households have air conditioners. Not that I'm shocked, but only because I ...
items.content:
Only about 25% of French households have air conditioners. Not that I'm shocked, but only because I already knew that European citizens are forced to take a shower only a couple of times a week and wear sweaters at home in the winter.

No sane person without the Stockholm syndrome would accept living in such conditions in 2026.

European bureaucrats and left-wing politicians are working and living in air-conditioned spaces, but the average European citizen lives in a medieval castle.

All hotels I ever stayed in Europe have air conditioners, because why would anyone respect the aborigine, right?

https://t.co/dSm1uk6oV9

items.title: The one thing insufferable about Europeans is they usually can't admit they have a problem when they...
items.content:
The one thing insufferable about Europeans is they usually can't admit they have a problem when they have it and will keep showing outlier cases to justify their point

The % of buildings in Europe with AC installed in 2026 is still just 20%!

It's 90% in the US, which means literally almost every single place you enter has AC blowing cold air

The honest answer here would be:
> yes we don't have enough AC, let's install it everywhere, it will definitely improve our thinking and society!

From 2020 to 2025, 248,875 people died in Europe due to heat-related deaths

During the same 2020 to...
items.content:
From 2020 to 2025, 248,875 people died in Europe due to heat-related deaths

During the same 2020 to 2025 timeframe, just 9,436 people died from heat-related deaths in the United States, or 26x less!

The self-righteous Europeans who don't want to install AC are literally killing their grandparents and other vulnerable people

items.title:
@MojDedko That's false

"Air conditioning (AC) penetration in Central Europe is relatively low, typi...
items.content:
@MojDedko That's false

"Air conditioning (AC) penetration in Central Europe is relatively low, typically ranging between 10% and 25% for private households. This lags far behind regions like the US (90%) but is rising rapidly as intense summer heatwaves become more common across the continent. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]"

Especially countries like Austria and even Switzerland are actively anti-AC 

Today the Swiss canton of Luzern had to take off their clothes because it was too hot inside, they wouldn't use AC

https://t.co/Upl9oRkONC

items.content_html:
@el_rei_gabriel I live on the beach in Portugal and no we need AC at night to get the room down to 18'C

Modern houses are insulated so that they radiate heat with a delay of 12 hours, so that the sun heat from the day is used to keep the house warm at night

That makes it about 25'C in the night
